includedSources.name: The Dark Side of the Boom

items.title: Jane Street $16.4B Q1 (+122% YoY), HRT $6.4B (+135%) — nonbank trading Q2 looks even bigger
items.idea: Jane Street and HRT generated $16.4B and $6.4B in Q1 2026, up 122% and 135% YoY respectively. Compare: Goldman Sachs equity intermediation was up just 7% YoY in Q1. The gap is structural, not cyclical — it is about who owns the retail ecosystem. Citadel Securities executes ~35% of all US-listed retail volumes. Retail cash equity volumes in May ran 60% above the 2025 average and more than twice the 2024 average — Q2 for the nonbanks may be even more extreme.
items.angle: The real story of market structure in 2026 is the nonbank capture of retail flow. While banks report double-digit trading growth, the nonbanks are putting up triple-digit numbers — and the gap is widening. Trade: long market structure beneficiaries, short bank trading narratives.
items.tweetA: Jane Street: $16.4B in one quarter, up 122% YoY. Goldman equity trading: up 7%. This is not a good quarter vs a bad one. It is a structural shift. The retail ecosystem now runs through firms you have never heard of, not the banks you know. The gap is widening, not closing.
items.tweetB: The most interesting numbers in Q1 2026 did not come from any S&P 500 company. Jane Street: $16.4B revenue. HRT: $6.4B. Both up 120%+. Both private. Both dwarfing Goldman's entire trading franchise. The question nobody asks: when do they go public?

items.title: May retail equity volumes broke 2021 records by 10%; June running 9% above that — 9 of 10 largest retail trading days ever happened in the last month
items.idea: Citadel Securities data via Scott Rubner: May shattered previous activity records in cash equities, surpassing the January 2021 high by more than 10%. Retail volumes ran 60% above the 2025 average. June is running 9% above May. June 12 marked the largest single-day retail net buying ever, surpassing the previous record by 50%. Semiconductor options daily premium running 5x historical average in May, up another 16% in June. Unlike 2021 meme stock era, retail is now concentrated in the same AI/semi names institutions are chasing.
items.angle: This is institutional-retail convergence, not 2021 redux. When leveraged multi-strat hedge funds and retail investors are long identical positions, the unwind is violent and correlated. The music is playing and everyone is dancing — that is the risk, not the opportunity.
items.tweetA: June 12: largest single-day retail net buying ever. Previous record beaten by 50%. Semiconductor options running 5x historical average. 9 of 10 largest retail trading days ever happened in the last month. Retail is now chasing exactly what institutions are long. That is not strength. That is fragility.
items.tweetB: Citadel Securities data: retail is not in meme stocks anymore. They are in the exact same AI and semiconductor names as the largest leveraged hedge funds. Correlation between multi-strat funds and market moves is at an all-time high. Everyone is long the same trade. The music keeps playing. Until it does not.

items.title: US Commerce ordered Anthropic to halt global model exports — first-ever AI export controls; Mistral pivots to sovereignty argument
items.idea: US Commerce officials ordered Anthropic to halt global exports of its Fable 5 model over national security concerns tied to foreign military use. This is the first use of export powers against an AI company. Mistral immediately turned it into a marketing event: US controls strengthen the case for open-weight models and European AI sovereignty. Europe's AI strategy now increasingly focuses on reducing reliance on external providers before dependency deepens.
items.angle: AI export controls are the chip export controls of 2026 — targeting software rather than hardware. This accelerates European and Chinese domestic model development. Mistral is the most obvious beneficiary narrative. Watch for EU AI sovereignty spending to increase.
items.tweetA: The US just issued the first-ever export controls on an AI model. Anthropic's Fable 5 is now restricted. If you thought chip controls were the end of the AI supply chain fight, the game just moved up the stack to the models themselves. Every government without a domestic frontier model is now running the math.
items.tweetB: Anthropic restricted from global exports. Mistral's response in under 24 hours: this is why you need open-weight models and EU sovereignty. They are not wrong. One US regulatory action just handed the European AI argument to every politician on the continent.

items.title: Seed stage pipeline is actively contracting: new financings no longer replace shutdowns; Series A graduation below post-2021 norms
items.idea: Lightspeed's Nnamdi Iregbulem: new Seed financings are no longer replacing startups that exit through shutdowns or acquisitions, shrinking the overall early-stage pipeline. Graduation rates to Series A remain below post-2021 levels, concentrating capital into a narrower set of companies. The funnel is narrowing from the bottom — not just slower, but structurally smaller.
items.angle: This has second-order effects: fewer seeds today means fewer Series A in 18 months, fewer growth-stage companies in 3 years. If this persists, the next generation of breakout companies is being underfunded right now.
items.tweetA: Seed funding is not just slow. The pipeline is actively shrinking. New formations no longer replace shutdowns. Series A graduation below any post-2021 cohort. The venture funnel is narrowing from the bottom — and the effects compound upward with a 2-3 year lag.
items.tweetB: The hidden VC story: AI made it cheaper to start a company and simultaneously made it harder to get funded. The bar for what constitutes a defensible seed-stage business just moved up. Fewer companies are clearing it. Lightspeed is tracking the contraction in real time.

items.title: OpenAI burned $3.7B in Q1 2026 while preparing for IPO — annualized $15B burn against $30B ARR
items.idea: OpenAI reportedly spent $3.7B in Q1 2026 while scaling revenue and infrastructure. Annualized, that is ~$15B in burn against even a $30B ARR figure. The company went from $3B to $30B ARR in one year. The IPO will price a narrative requiring simultaneous revenue growth AND margin expansion at scale to persist indefinitely.
items.angle: McDonald's does $25B in annual revenue and generates significant free cash flow. OpenAI does $30B ARR and burns $15B/year. The IPO will be the most consequential pricing event for AI valuations since Nvidia's run began.
items.tweetA: OpenAI: $3.7B in one quarter. Annualized: $15B in burn. Revenue grew from $3B to $30B ARR in one year. The bull case requires both to work simultaneously, forever, at scale. The IPO will price that probability. That number will tell us more about AI valuations than any earnings report.
items.tweetB: $30B ARR. $15B annual burn. No free cash flow. Preparing for a massive IPO. The argument is that infrastructure spend now creates defensible margin later. Maybe. But we have heard that story before. The question is whether capital markets stay this patient.

items.title: Jake and Logan Paul launch $100M hard tech fund targeting robotics, defense, chips, AI infrastructure
items.idea: Jake Paul and Logan Paul launched a $100M fund targeting robotics, defense, chips, and AI infrastructure. Also notable: AVP and Earlybird closed a €500M European Dual-Use and Defence Growth Fund; Google and Monashees launched a GAMA Fund for Brazil's AI-first startups. Creator-capital is entering the defense and hard-tech stack via new vehicle structures.
items.angle: This is a distribution story more than an investment thesis. A $100M fund with 10M+ followers as an LP recruitment pipeline is a genuinely different model. The LP composition matters more than the fund size.
items.tweetA: Jake and Logan Paul: $100M fund. Robotics, defense, chips, AI infrastructure. Laugh at it or not — these two have beaten more predictions than they have lost. Audience is the new carry. Defense tech just found a distribution channel nobody expected.
items.tweetB: Creator to influencer to entrepreneur to VC. The Paul brothers go hard tech: $100M fund, defense and robotics focus. Same week: €500M EU dual-use defence fund from Earlybird. Capital is flowing into the defence-AI stack from every direction simultaneously.

items.title: Z.ai open-sources GLM-5.2: 1M-token context, MIT license, works natively with Claude Code, Cline, and Cursor
items.idea: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an open-weights frontier model with a usable 1-million-token context window, up to 128K output tokens, MIT-licensed and natively compatible with Claude Code, Cline, OpenCode, Roo Code, and Goose. Two effort levels: max (peak performance) and high (quality-vs-token efficiency). Tuned for long-horizon coding, agentic work, full-repository state management.
items.angle: The closed-model premium continues to compress. An MIT-licensed frontier model compatible with every major coding agent is free infrastructure for the entire ecosystem. The models-as-commodities dynamic is accelerating.
items.tweetA: Z.ai dropped a frontier model: 1M token context, 128K output, MIT license, works out of the box with Claude Code, Cursor, and Cline. The infrastructure cost for agentic coding just dropped again. How many more of these before the inference premium argument breaks completely?
items.tweetB: GLM-5.2: 1M token context, open weights, free. Six months ago this would have been a headline model. Today it ships as a free alternative to closed APIs. The capability floor is rising faster than the cost floor. The gap between frontier and open-source is the tightest it has ever been.

items.title: Block's Builderbot writes 15% of all company code — joining Amazon (25%), Microsoft (30%), Google (25%)
items.idea: Block (Jack Dorsey's fintech company) reports its internal AI coding agent, Builderbot, is now responsible for 15% of all code written at the company. This sits alongside Amazon (25%), Microsoft (30%), and Google (25% of new code AI-assisted). The enterprise AI code adoption curve is consistent and accelerating across different tech stacks and company cultures.
items.angle: These numbers compound. 15% today becomes 30-40% in 18 months if the trend holds. The productivity argument is no longer theoretical — it is in earnings calls and internal reports.
items.tweetA: Block's Builderbot writes 15% of the company's code. Amazon: 25%. Google: 25%. Microsoft: 30%. This is not a feature. It is a productivity curve that compounds. The question nobody is asking out loud: what happens to headcount plans when this hits 50%?
items.tweetB: AI writes X% of our code is quickly becoming the new we have a mobile app. Block: 15%. The companies not disclosing a number either do not have one or do not like what it shows. This metric will show up in every enterprise software company's strategy deck within 12 months.

items.source: Phil Dauber / Morning Musings; David Lin Report
items.title: Warsh FOMC: 0 to 9 officials penciling a hike in one meeting; 2Y +14bps to 4.19%; dollar at 1-year high — regime change
items.idea: Kevin Warsh's debut FOMC: 9 of 19 officials now pencil in a hike by year-end, up from zero in March. The 2Y jumped 14bps to 4.19%, the dollar hit a one-year high. Warsh launched 5 task forces to review Fed communication practices — signaling less transparency and more discretion. Sri-Kumar predicts 3 hikes by December, seeding stagflation in 2027. Bianco: bond investors stop panicking when the Fed starts panicking.
items.angle: The regime change is not just the hike dots — it is the opacity signal. When the Fed stops telegraphing via forward guidance, the terminal rate becomes a genuine unknown again. Long vol is the structural trade in a less-transparent Fed regime.
items.tweetA: Zero FOMC members penciled a hike in March. Nine do now. Warsh's first meeting moved the committee further than any single meeting since 2022. And the market bought it. The market said prove it. PCE prints Thursday. That is the proof.
items.tweetB: Warsh is dropping forward guidance, launching task forces, signaling opacity. From a volatility perspective this is not just hawkish — it is a regime change. When the Fed stops telegraphing, the terminal rate becomes an unknown. VIX at 16.78 during the meeting where nuclear talks collapsed is not calm. It is mispriced.

items.title: Accenture -18% record single-day drop: FY guidance cut to 3-4% — the starkest public signal that AI is eating consulting
items.idea: Accenture cut FY guidance to 3-4% local currency growth ex-federal work, producing a record single-day decline of 18%. The starkest signal yet that AI is actively cannibalizing traditional consulting. The infrastructure winners — semis, hyperscalers — are thriving. The incumbents who got paid to implement software are getting repriced by the software itself.
items.angle: ACN is the canary for the entire knowledge-work consulting economy. The revenue model of turning human judgment into billable hours is being structurally disrupted. If you are building DCFs on consulting firms, rethink the terminal growth assumption.
items.tweetA: Accenture: -18% in a single day. FY guidance: 3-4% growth. AI is not coming for consulting. It is here. The firms paid to implement software are being disrupted by the software itself. $ACN This is not a bad quarter. It is a structural repricing.
items.tweetB: AI is eating consulting before it eats most other things. Reason: consulting is converting human judgment into output. That is exactly what LLMs do, faster and cheaper. $ACN -18%. The capex for the winners has not even fully landed yet. The consulting repricing is only in the early innings.

items.title: MU reports Tuesday — $34.5B revenue (+270% YoY); 81% gross margin on HBM is the only number that matters
items.idea: Micron reports Tuesday. Street consensus: $34.5B revenue, +270% YoY. The actual number to watch is gross margin at ~81% — this is the HBM pricing power verdict. Structural premium or cyclical spike? A beat broadens the AI hardware rally to memory and foundry. A miss concentrates it back to NVDA alone.
items.angle: If MU delivers 81%+ GM, it confirms HBM as a structural premium and opens the door for Samsung/SK Hynix read-throughs. Binary event with clear implications for the entire non-NVDA AI hardware stack.
items.tweetA: $MU reports Tuesday. Not watching revenue ($34.5B, +270% YoY — that is just consensus). Watching gross margin at 81%. That is the HBM pricing power verdict: structural or cyclical. The entire non-NVDA AI hardware thesis hangs on one number.
items.tweetB: +270% revenue growth and the question is whether that is good enough. That is what a stretched AI multiple buys you. 81% gross margin or the rally stalls. A beat broadens the trade. A miss takes it back to NVDA alone. Reports Tuesday. Binary.

items.title: $119.2B into US equity funds in one week — largest ever; mid-cap set its largest-ever weekly inflow record
items.idea: US equity funds attracted a record $119.2B in the week through June 17. Mid-cap posted its largest-ever weekly inflow. Small-caps their second-largest. Record inflows into an untested conviction trade is not strength — it is fragility with a lag.
items.angle: Contrarian signal: record inflows on top of zero data confirmation creates structural fragility. Asymmetry heading into PCE: -150 points on a hot print, +100 on a soft one.
items.tweetA: $119.2B into US equity funds in one week. Mid-cap: largest-ever weekly inflow. Record inflows into a conviction trade with zero data confirmation. That is not strength, that is fragility with a lag. PCE prints Thursday. The bet just got larger at exactly the wrong time.
items.tweetB: Record equity inflows the week before the PCE print that determines whether the Fed hikes. Asymmetry: -150 points on hot PCE, +100 on soft. The math does not favor adding here.

items.title: BTC at $62,589 with $2.97B cumulative ETF outflows — neither safe-haven nor risk-on anymore
items.idea: Bitcoin at $62,589 with $2.97B in cumulative ETF outflows. No safe-haven bid when geopolitics escalates, no risk-on bid when equities rally. The digital gold thesis was already dead, now the risk-on thesis is dying too. BTC described as an orphan asset class — persistent selling in a regime that has no narrative use for it.
items.angle: The narrative vacuum is more dangerous than any price level. Assets without a working narrative have no floor below the cost basis of the weakest hands.
items.tweetA: $BTC at $62,589. $2.97B cumulative ETF outflows. Iran war escalates — crypto does not rally. Equities rip — crypto does not follow. The digital gold thesis is dead. The risk-on thesis is dying. What is the narrative that makes someone buy today?
items.tweetB: The most revealing thing about crypto right now is not the price. It is the narrative vacuum. Geopolitical bid: no. Risk-on bid: no. Just $2.97B of ETF outflows and persistent selling. Bianco: trough of disillusionment. Stablecoins are the only utility case that is working.

items.title: PCE Thursday: Wells Fargo expects 4.1% annualized vs Fed's own 3.6% forecast — asymmetry is -150/+100 on S&P
items.idea: Wells Fargo forecasts May PCE at +0.5% m/m, pushing annualized rate to 4.1% against the Fed's own 3.6% year-end projection. Hot print = Fed's own dots look optimistic, September reopened for real. Dauber's S&P asymmetry: hot PCE → ~7,350 (-150 points); soft PCE → ~7,600 (+100 points).
items.angle: Expected value is negative given skewed payoff and a consensus positioned for the soft outcome. Only justified being long if you believe PCE comes in at or below 3.6%.
items.tweetA: Wells Fargo: PCE 4.1% annualized Thursday. Fed projects 3.6%. If Wells Fargo is right, the Fed's own dots look dovish. September is back on the table. The market just bet $119B on the soft outcome. Asymmetry: -150 points hot, +100 points soft. The math does not work.
items.tweetB: PCE Thursday. Hot print: S&P 7,350. Soft print: 7,600. Risking 150 to make 100 heading into record inflows and an un-tested conviction trade. VIX at 16.78. Vol is underpriced.

items.title: INTC +10.6% on Apple fab deal announced by Trump via Truth Social — neither company confirmed; 18A in production June 16
items.idea: Intel surged 10.6% after Trump announced on Truth Social that Apple would manufacture chips on Intel's 18A process node. Intel's 18A started production June 16, making timing plausible. Neither Apple nor Intel confirmed. The move is pricing it as done. Risk/reward asymmetric to the downside until a principal confirms.
items.angle: If real, transformational for Intel fab turnaround. If walked back, half the move returns immediately. The positioning question: betting on a social media post or on the underlying 18A thesis?
items.tweetA: $INTC +10.6%. Catalyst: a Trump Truth Social post about an Apple chip deal. Neither Apple nor Intel confirmed. 18A started production June 16 so timing is plausible. That is the entire trade until someone says yes or no publicly.
items.tweetB: Trump announced an Apple-Intel deal via social media. Intel: no comment. Apple: no comment. Market: +10.6%. We are pricing unconfirmed industrial policy from a social media post at 10-figure market cap moves. This is 2026.

items.title: CLARITY Act ready for full Senate vote; Show Me era permanently resets crypto credibility standards
items.idea: GENIUS Act (stablecoin legislation) already passed. CLARITY Act (comprehensive market structure legislation) ready for full Senate vote — if passed, founders can publicly discuss specifics previously not legally possible. BlackRock, Fidelity, JP Morgan, Franklin Templeton all building at scale. Vision-to-proof ratio permanently shifted: was 80/20 in 2021, now reversed. New proof stack requires mainnet data, real transaction volumes, real partnerships.
items.angle: CLARITY passage unlocks institutional product launches waiting for regulatory clarity. The inflection point for serious institutional crypto infrastructure.
items.tweetA: The CLARITY Act is ready for a full Senate vote. If it passes, founders can publicly discuss what they are building with a legal specificity that was not possible before. BlackRock tokenized treasuries. Fidelity built custody. Franklin Templeton launched an onchain money market fund. The products exist. Now the law catches up.
items.tweetB: a16z crypto's thesis: the Show Me era is permanent, not cyclical. 80% vision and 20% substance worked in 2021. That ratio has flipped. The highest-quality builders benefit most. CLARITY Act is the regulatory unlock. The institutions already built the products.

items.title: US-Iran war 100+ days in: Brent $112, WTI $109, both +50% since Feb 28 — Pakistan MOU fragile; Hormuz flows far below normal
items.idea: US-Iran war ongoing since February 28. Brent at $112.10 (+50%), WTI at $108.66. Pakistan-mediated MOU would extend ceasefire 60 days and partially reopen Hormuz — but Iran conditioning talks on Israel stopping Hezbollah operations, structurally impossible for US to deliver. 12.5M barrels transited Hormuz June 18 (pre-war: 130 ships/day). Hanke: war cost $500B, US deficit from $2T to $2.5T, Iran holds de facto Hormuz toll.
items.angle: MOU is structurally fragile. Iran gets leverage, US gets face-saving exit. Physical flows have not returned to normal. Market is pricing optimism; physics is pricing something else.
items.tweetA: 100+ days of US-Iran war. Brent $112, WTI $109 — up 50% since February 28. A Pakistan-mediated MOU gives Iran de facto control over Hormuz flows. Goldman: +$10/barrel for every additional month of closure. The market is pricing peace. The physical flows have not normalized.
items.tweetB: Hormuz before the war: 130 ships per day. Hormuz June 18: 12.5M barrels total. Partially reopened is doing enormous diplomatic work. The MOU is a 60-day stall. Hanke: greatest strategic catastrophe in American history.

items.title: Goldman: +$10/barrel per month of Hormuz closure; 1/3 of global seaborne fertilizer disrupted; China imports cut 2.7M bpd; Asia pivoting to coal
items.idea: Goldman Sachs: +$10/barrel to year-end price for each additional month of Hormuz closure. US diesel above $5/gallon. ~1/3 of global seaborne fertilizer trade constrained. Adrian Day: China cut oil imports from 11.7M to 9M bpd partly because physical supply was unavailable. Asian importers (Japan, Indonesia, Malaysia) shifting to coal. Every major oil spike in 80 years preceded a recession.
items.angle: The fertilizer-to-food price chain is underreported — 3-6 month lag to food prices. Coal demand surging in Asia has its own implications. The market watches oil; the second-order trades are fertilizer, agriculture, and coal.
items.tweetA: Goldman: +$10/barrel for every month Hormuz stays closed. We are 100+ days in. One-third of global seaborne fertilizer trade is disrupted. Nobody is writing the food price piece yet. The agricultural read-through is 3-6 months behind the oil read-through.
items.tweetB: China cut oil imports from 11.7M to 9M bpd. Part of that was not a choice — they simply could not get the oil. Japan, Indonesia, Malaysia are switching to coal. US energy independence insulates North America. Europe and Asia are facing a different equation.

items.title: 30-year Treasury touched 5.197% — highest since July 2007; 62% of fund managers expect 6%; 5.25% is the equity valuation trigger
items.idea: 30-year Treasury briefly touched 5.197% (highest since July 2007), 10-year at 4.687% (highest since January 2025). BofA survey: 62% of global fund managers expect 30-year to reach 6%. BMO's Ian Lyngen: 5.25% on the 30-year triggers a more durable pullback in equity valuations. Bianco's rule: bond investors stop panicking when the Fed starts panicking (cutting). Fed just moved toward hiking.
items.angle: 5.197% is a technical level. 5.25% is the cited trigger for equity re-rating — 5bps away. If 62% of fund managers are right about 6%, duration is the short of the decade.
items.tweetA: 30-year Treasury: 5.197%, highest since July 2007. 62% of global fund managers expect 6%. BMO says 5.25% triggers a more durable pullback in equity valuations. We are 5 basis points away from that trigger. The most important chart is not the S&P.
items.tweetB: The 30-year yield has not been this high since the summer before the iPhone launched. Bianco: bond investors stop panicking when the Fed starts panicking (cutting). The Fed just penciled in hikes. The panic has not started. Neither has the bond rally.

items.title: Hanke: money supply accelerating 18 months; 80% from commercial bank lending; Warsh is a monetarist light — potential Volcker parallel
items.idea: Steve Hanke: money supply accelerating 18 months driven mostly by commercial bank lending (~80% of money creation). CPI at 4.2% — double the 2% target. Bank deregulation could expand lending and keep inflation uncorked. Warsh a monetarist light: unlike Powell who rejected the quantity theory of money, Warsh has not. If Warsh embraces MV=PY as Volcker did, this is a genuine paradigm shift.
items.angle: The monetarist lens changes the inflation read entirely. If credit creation is the driver (not just energy), a Hormuz ceasefire does not solve inflation. The deeper problem is in bank balance sheets and regulatory stance.
items.tweetA: Money supply accelerating 18 months. 80% driven by commercial bank lending. CPI at 4.2% — double the target. Hanke: the Fed does not look at money supply. Warsh might. He has not rejected the quantity theory of money like Powell did. A monetarist Fed Chair would change everything.
items.tweetB: The central bank story is not rates. It is the plumbing. 80% of money creation comes from commercial bank lending. If you loosen bank regulation while hiking rates, you press gas and brake simultaneously. Warsh knows this. The question is which pedal he chooses.

items.source: David Lin Report / Gary Wagner / World Gold Council
items.title: 57% of central banks plan to actively accumulate gold over the coming year — structurally different from retail or hedge fund flows
items.idea: World Gold Council survey: 57% of central bank respondents plan to actively accumulate gold over the coming year, repositioning reserves away from fiat currencies. Gold at ~$4,300, down 20% from highs above $5,500. Net central bank purchases at highest quarterly level since late 2024 — during the price correction. Wagner: draws George Soros-in-2012 parallel — Soros buying at the 2012 bottom set the floor; central banks are playing that role now.
items.angle: Central bank accumulation at 57% is a structural multi-year bid. Unlike retail or hedge fund flows, central banks do not chase price or panic-sell. The correction is creating the buying opportunity they need.
items.tweetA: 57% of central banks plan to actively accumulate gold over the next year. Not considering. Actively accumulating. Gold is down 20% from highs at $4,300. Net CB purchases hit their highest quarterly level in this move — during the correction. The buyers with the deepest pockets are buying the dip.
items.tweetB: When the institution that issues fiat currency is selling it to buy gold, you have your signal. 57% of central banks. World Gold Council survey. This is structurally different from retail or hedge fund buying — these are sovereign reserve managers. They do not panic-sell.

charts.desc: World Gold Council chart on central bank purchase intentions — worth opening

items.title: Hormuz closure shifting Asia from oil to coal; every major oil spike in 80 years preceded recession; ECB hike called a policy mistake
items.idea: Adrian Day: Asian importers (Japan, Indonesia, Malaysia) actively shifting to coal due to Hormuz supply uncertainty. Oil at $83/barrel lowest 20th percentile inflation-adjusted over 20 years — strain falls on lower-income populations. Every oil price spike in 80 years preceded a recession. North America insulated; Day expects European and parts of Asian economies to slip into recession if conflict drags. Sri-Kumar called ECB hike a policy mistake, echoing its 2008 blunder.
items.angle: Coal demand surge in Asia has environmental and energy policy implications not yet priced. If Europe enters recession while hiking rates, ECB is in an impossible position.
items.tweetA: Every major oil price spike over the past 80 years preceded a recession. North America has energy independence. Europe and Asia do not. Asian importers are pivoting to coal because Hormuz is unreliable. The ECB just hiked. Sri-Kumar: policy mistake, echoing 2008.
items.tweetB: The Hormuz closure has a coal footnote. Japan, Indonesia, Malaysia are switching fuel sources. That is an emissions story, a coal demand story, and an energy security story all at once. The oil market gets the headlines. The coal market gets the contracts.

items.title: AI stocks now 48% of S&P 500 — railroad-era concentration; during June selloff 400+ non-AI stocks rose while index fell 4.5%
items.idea: Jim Bianco: AI stocks now 48% of S&P 500, concentration unseen since the railroad era. During early-June selloff, index fell 4.5% while 400+ non-AI companies rose. Bianco places AI at 1997-98 on Gartner hype cycle — still bullish but warning of eventual 83% Nasdaq-style bust before technology proves transformative. Current constraint: too little compute, not too much.
items.angle: Equal-weight vs cap-weight is producing visible divergence in real time. The 97/98 analogy: still bullish for 2-3 years, but the bust eventually arrives at 83% off peak before transformation is proven.
items.tweetA: AI stocks are 48% of the S&P 500. Railroad-era concentration. Early June selloff: S&P -4.5%. 400+ non-AI stocks: positive. The index has stopped telling you about the market. Equal-weight is telling a completely different story right now.
items.tweetB: Bianco: AI is 1997, not 2000. Still bullish. But: history suggests an 83% Nasdaq-style bust before AI proves truly transformative — after the overbuild, before the maturity. The current constraint is too little compute, not too much. Capex raises are still rational. For now.

items.title: Bianco: AI's real use case is replacing the entire $300-400B SaaS market with a single context window
items.idea: Bianco: AI's real use case is not a new category — it is replacing the entire $300-400B annual SaaS market with one context window. Every point-solution SaaS maps to a task a capable AI handles in one session. On crypto: places it near trough of disillusionment at 15 years old, still mostly speculative. Redemption only if developers build genuine utility like stablecoins — already the dominant electronic dollar in dollarized Venezuela.
items.angle: Cloud consumption growth accelerates (positive for AWS, Azure, GCP) while SaaS app-layer valuations face compression. Stablecoins-as-EM-dollar is the most concrete crypto utility case anywhere in the world today.
items.tweetA: Bianco: AI's real use case is not a new product category. It is replacing the entire $300-400B SaaS market with one context window. Every point-solution SaaS becomes a prompt. The cloud infra layer wins. The application layer gets repriced. $MSFT $AMZN $GOOGL vs $CRM $SNOW $DDOG
items.tweetB: Crypto: 15 years old, mostly still speculative, trough of disillusionment. But stablecoins in dollarized Venezuela? Already the dominant electronic dollar. Utility redemption comes from one use case proving out completely, not the whole space at once. One use case is all it takes.

items.title: Gold's share of global savings at 0.5% — half the four-decade mean of 2%; setup reminds Rule of 1975 before the $850 run
items.idea: Rick Rule: gold's share of global savings near 0.5%, down from four-decade mean of 2%. Expects mean reversion. Current weakness from higher US nominal rates, recession fears, credit collapse worries. Setup reminds him most of 1975: rising rates cratered gold from $200 to $100 before loss of political nerve sent it toward $850. Iran conflict pushed US deficit from $2T to $2.5T, with massive refinancing needs ahead.
items.angle: The 0.5% vs 2% savings share gap is a powerful mean-reversion argument. Rule's 1975 parallel: current period maps to the tightening-crushed-gold phase, before the political nerve breaks.
items.tweetA: Gold's share of global savings: 0.5%. Four-decade mean: 2%. Rick Rule: reversion is inevitable. 1975: rising rates crushed gold from $200 to $100. Then political nerve broke and it ran to $850. We are in the crushed part. The question is how long it lasts.
items.tweetB: Rick Rule on the Iran war: added $500B to US costs. Deficit went from $2T to $2.5T. Massive refinancing coming. High long-term rates reflect private capital's skepticism toward the dollar. He saves in gold, invests separately. The distinction between savings vehicle and investment vehicle matters here.

items.title: Rosenberg: next Fed move is a CUT — trimmed-mean PCE at 2.3%, real wages down 3 months running, savings rate collapsed from 5% to 3%
items.idea: David Rosenberg minority view: (1) No deal — just MOU, oil fell only $4. (2) Core CPI May 0.2% below consensus; trimmed-mean PCE ~2.3%; strip out oil-adjacent and his core runs at 1.8%. (3) YoY payroll growth 0.3%, household survey negative, real wages contracted 3 consecutive months, real disposable income -1% YoY, savings rate from 5% to 3%. US consumer already in recession on the income side. He bought the 5% US long bond because nobody wanted it.
items.angle: Rosenberg vs consensus is the most asymmetric analytical bet. If trimmed-mean PCE at 2.3% is the right read, the bond market is badly mispriced and the 5% long bond is the trade of the year. The savings rate collapse is the tell.
items.tweetA: Rosenberg is buying the 5% US long bond because nobody else wants it. His case: trimmed-mean PCE is 2.3%, real wages contracted 3 months running, household survey is negative, savings rate already fell from 5% to 3%. The consensus sees inflation. He sees a consumer already in recession.
items.tweetB: Consensus: Fed hikes in September. Rosenberg: next move is a cut. Because real disposable income is down 1% YoY and spending stayed positive only because the savings rate collapsed from 5% to 3%. You cannot draw down savings forever. Spending vacuum in H2.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Europe is not missing the AI boom — it is missing the profit pool; Korea export surge no longer translates to Euro Stoxx earnings
items.idea: Simon White's key signal: Korea's AI-led export surge no longer translating into stronger Euro Stoxx earnings, breaking a 20-year relationship. The US captures the high-margin AI profit pool through hyperscalers, chips, cloud, data-center infrastructure. Korea/Taiwan offer supply-chain leverage through memory, foundries, advanced packaging. Europe investable selectively: ASML (monopoly), grid buildout, automation, power systems — not broad index exposure.
items.angle: Europe can rally tactically on valuation, but the AI earnings engine sits in the US and Asia. Broad European equity exposure in an AI supercycle will underperform. ASML and power/grid infrastructure is the right framing.
items.tweetA: Korea's AI export surge is real. It stopped translating into Euro Stoxx earnings. A 20-year relationship just broke. The reason: the US owns the high-margin AI profit pool — hyperscalers, chips, cloud, data center. Europe gets the demand. America gets the margin. $ASML is the exception.
items.tweetB: Europe can rally tactically on valuation. But the AI earnings engine sits in the US and Asia. Simon White: the Korea export-to-Euro Stoxx earnings correlation broke this cycle. Selective is the polite word for the situation: ASML, grid, power — not broad Euro index.

items.title: KCR: energy generating 8% FCF yield on $65 oil with 5% total yield — looks like NVDA did years ago; tech pays nothing
items.idea: Kailash Concepts Research: energy stocks generate 8% FCF yield at $65 oil with 5% total yield — versus tech stocks generating nominal FCF yield and paying nothing. Tech has highest % of companies beating revenue estimates; energy near the bottom. KCR: energy looks like NVDA and tech did years ago. Bull case is in front while investor focus is fixated on tech due to trailing performance. Classic extrapolation bias.
items.angle: The FCF yield comparison is the clearest expression of the value anomaly. 8% FCF yield on $65 oil (not the $112 war-price) is extraordinary for any sector. The mean reversion trade requires oil normalization.
items.tweetA: Energy stocks: 8% FCF yield at $65 oil. 5% total yield. Tech: nominal FCF, pays you nothing. KCR: energy looks like NVDA did years ago. Bull case is in front, not behind. Most investors are fixated on the rearview mirror. $XOM $CVX $VLO $MPC
items.tweetB: The extrapolation bias runs both ways. Tech gets credit for trailing performance. Energy gets punished for it. Same cognitive error, different direction. KCR is pointing at the same mistake that made NVDA look like a value trap in 2019. History does not repeat but the bias does.

charts.desc: KCR table showing energy vs tech FCF yields and valuation spread — Charts inside, worth opening

items.title: Kuppy: data centers are the new Shale — buyback-to-equity-issuance pivot is the key signpost
items.idea: Harris Kupperman draws the data center = shale parallel at the capital cycle level. The pivot from buybacks to equity issuances among hyperscalers is the key signpost: not the end of the buildout, but the first clear warning. Quote: tech bros are chasing AGI just like oilmen dream about drilling wells. It will take a lot of pain to talk them out of this. The question is not whether the buildout continues but when the re-rating starts.
items.angle: Shale peak involved $180B annual capex, massive equity dilution, then crash to 30% of peak activity before productivity gains were absorbed. If data centers follow the same arc, equity returns get horrific before the technology proves out.
items.tweetA: Kuppy: data centers are the new Shale. The signal is not the capex. It is the pivot from buybacks to equity issuances. When hyperscalers switch from returning capital to raising it, that is Act II of the Shale story. We may be there. The re-rating starts when investors get disgusted.
items.tweetB: The Shale analogy is not about the technology. It is about the capital cycle. Oilmen kept drilling as returns collapsed because they were chasing reserves. Tech operators are chasing AGI. They will not stop voluntarily. The pain has to do it for them. Kuppy: Act II has started.

items.title: Schwab tightening margin requirements for retail long-short strategies — when the house gets nervous, should we?
items.idea: Charles Schwab imposing new margin requirements for clients using long-short investment strategies due to counterparty and balance-sheet risk if leveraged positions move sharply. Schwab's move creates mechanical deleveraging pressure: simultaneous long selling and short covering. Timing: during record equity fund inflows. Greenberg: when the house gets nervous at equity ATHs, should we?
items.angle: Prime broker getting nervous is a late-cycle signal that precedes client concern. Creates mechanical deleveraging pressure that is regulatory/risk-management driven, not sentiment-driven.
items.tweetA: Schwab just tightened margin requirements on retail long-short strategies because of counterparty and balance-sheet risk. Translation: we are nervous about sharp moves. The house is getting nervous at equity ATHs and record inflows. That gap between the house's read and the client's read has one resolution.
items.tweetB: The broker gets scared before the client does. Always. Schwab: tightening margin on leveraged retail strategies. When the house gets nervous, should we? This is the kind of signal you miss when you are focused on flow data instead of the risk manager's behavior.

items.title: Top 10 US stocks = $25.3T — larger than China's GDP; top 5 outweigh every non-US stock market combined; inverse correlation with next-decade returns
items.idea: Top 10 American companies: $25.3T combined — larger than China's entire $19.4T GDP and all of China's stock market, ~80% of US GDP. Top 5 at $18.6T outweigh every stock market outside the US combined (MSCI China = $11.8T). Historical pattern: extreme concentration correlates inversely with next decade's returns. Nifty Fifty: -45% into 1973-74 bear. Cap-weighted S&P 2000-2013: flat. Equal-weighted version compounded ahead the entire way.
items.angle: Equal-weight vs cap-weight is the most empirically grounded rotation trade on the board. The Nifty Fifty and 2000 both mark the precise moment equal-weight began multi-year outperformance. $RSP vs $SPY is the structural trade of the next decade.
items.tweetA: Top 10 US stocks: $25.3 trillion. That is larger than China's entire GDP. Larger than all of China's stock market. Top 5: bigger than every stock market outside the US combined. The last time we saw this level of concentration, they were called the Nifty Fifty. Then they fell 45%.
items.tweetB: Equal-weight vs cap-weight is the trade of the next decade. 1973: Nifty Fifty. 2000: cap-weighted. Both times extreme concentration marked the turning point. The equal-weighted version of the same index compounded ahead for years afterward. Same companies. Different weights. Different outcome. $RSP

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING: US market cap concentration chart — top 10 vs China GDP, top 5 vs rest of world

items.title: Dollar setup rhymes with 2014-2016 surge that sent crude -70%; every 10% DXY rise cuts 2-4% off S&P earnings
items.idea: DXY bounced cleanly off a long-term weekly trend line respected for over a decade, approaching resistance at 100.5, above its 50-week MA. Setup rhymes with 2020/21 consolidation that preceded a powerful move higher. 2014-2016 analog: dollar ripped from ~80 to 100 (+25%), crude collapsed from $100 to below $30 (-70%), EM equities hemorrhaged. Rule: every sustained 10% DXY rise carves 2-4% off S&P 500 earnings, concentrated in the most global names.
items.angle: Dollar breakout through 100.5 simultaneously pressures gold, oil, and EM equities. The irony: hawkish Fed drives dollar higher, which helps reduce inflation through commodity prices — but earnings estimates for multinationals compress in the meantime.
items.tweetA: DXY is coiling off a decade-long trend line, approaching 100.5 resistance. Run the 2014-2016 playbook if it breaks: dollar +25%, crude -70%, EM equities hemorrhaged. Every 10% DXY rise historically cuts 2-4% off S&P earnings. This is the tail risk that nobody is hedging against right now.
items.tweetB: The hardest thing to price: a hawkish Fed drives the dollar higher while Hormuz keeps oil elevated. These forces move in opposite directions. When they reconcile — oil down, dollar up — the speed will be violent. 100.5 on DXY is the line. One side of that trade is very crowded.

charts.desc: Dollar weekly chart with decade-long trend line and 100.5 resistance — Charts inside, worth opening

items.title: The chart that broke a 20-year relationship: America has been shrinking for 25 years measured in gold
items.idea: Macro Mornings central thesis: 9 charts in 48-hour window tell one story in sequence. First 5 are unambiguously bullish — US market dominance at record, Fed signaling resolve, dollar set up to break higher. But the final chart measures the US not in dollars but in gold. Measured in gold: America has been shrinking for 25 years. The narrative of US dominance is a dollar-denominated costume. The underlying purchasing-power story is very different. This is the chart almost no one is discussing that sits at the center of everything.
items.angle: The most powerful reframing in the current macro environment. Dollar strength makes everything look fine. Gold-deflated metrics expose the ongoing debasement since 2000. When 57% of central banks are accumulating gold, they are all reading the same chart.
items.tweetA: S&P at all-time highs in dollars. Measured in gold? America has been shrinking for 25 years. The costume is the dollar. Underneath, a different story. When 57% of central banks are actively accumulating gold, they are not making a speculative bet. They are reading the chart nobody wants to show.
items.tweetB: The most uncomfortable slide in any macro presentation: the S&P 500 measured in gold from 2000 to today. The gains look very different. Rick Rule: the S&P gains since 2000 look far less impressive once purchasing power is subtracted. The dollar is not a measuring stick. It is one of the things being measured.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING: 9-chart debasement sequence including US-in-gold chart. The full sequence is the argument.

items.source: David Lin Report / Peter Boettke (George Mason)
items.title: Capitalism favorability slipped to 54% (Gallup); socialism at 39%; sharpest divide among under-40s — cronyism is the mechanism
items.idea: George Mason economist Peter Boettke cites Gallup: capitalism's favorability slipped to 54%, socialism at 39%, sharpest divide among under-40s with no lived memory of communism's collapse. Two causes: (1) presentism — every crisis since 9/11 met by government intervention; (2) cronyism — government favoritism makes the system feel rigged, breeds zero-sum thinking. Boettke: declining social mobility links to cronyism (Raj Chetty's work), not markets per se.
items.angle: The 54/39 split is the electoral and policy backdrop for every wealth tax, nationalization proposal, and regulation-as-strategy move in the next decade. Understanding it as driven by cronyism (fixable) vs markets (unfixable) changes the political trajectory analysis.
items.tweetA: Capitalism: 54% favorable. Socialism: 39%. Among under-40s the gap is narrower still. Nobody under 40 has lived through communism's collapse. They have lived through 9/11, the financial crisis, and COVID — all met with government as the answer. You reap what you sow.
items.tweetB: Boettke's insight: the cronyism problem is being charged to the market's account. When the system looks rigged for connected insiders, people do not want less crony capitalism. They want something that sounds different. That preference shows up as socialism at 39% in Gallup. That is a political tail risk for every corporate earnings model.

items.title: S&P margins forecast 14.5% to 16.7% by 2027 — but half of last decade's returns already came from margin/multiple expansion that cannot repeat
items.idea: S&P 500 net profit margins at 14.5%, forecast to expand to 16.7% by 2027. But profit margins are the most mean-reverting series in finance. Roughly half of everything the S&P delivered over the past decade came from margins and multiples widening — a tailwind that cannot blow again from a record base. A move from 14.5% to 16.7% is a near-record expansion stacked on top of already near-record margins. This is the load-bearing wall of the entire bull case.
items.angle: The margin expansion story is the most specific risk in the bull case. Not enough to say AI will help — the question is whether it delivers measurable margin expansion within a 2-year DCF window. If AI delivers 2027 margins at 16.7%, valuations are justified. If they mean-revert, re-rating is severe.
items.tweetA: S&P margins: 14.5% today, forecast 16.7% by 2027. That is the bull case's load-bearing wall. Problem: half of the last decade's S&P returns came from margin expansion that has already happened. Stacking another 2.2 points from a record base requires AI to actually show up in the P&L within 24 months.
items.tweetB: Profit margins are the most mean-reverting series in finance. The consensus is forecasting record margins expand further from here. That is the bet. If AI delivers measurable cost reduction by 2027, it works. If not, this is precisely where the valuation math snaps. No soft landing for that.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING: S&P net profit margin trajectory 14.5% to 16.7% — the most optimistic assumption in the whole bull case
